figures.caption: FIG. 6. Power scaling ḡ of parallel vs. serial gates. Purple squares represent parallel, green circles represent serial. (a) Simulations with N = 13. For parallel, we consider N sets of N−1 2 gates, each set being parallelized. For serial, all pairs are considered. The largest power solution for parallel and serial is plotted. One-to-one power-time tradeoff is observed for both implementations. (b) Power requirements as a function of the number of ions. Pulses were a fixed duration synthesized with τ = 300µs. Only the largest power requirements are reported; for serial implementation we consider all N(N−1)

FIG. 7. Average pulse power 〈ḡ〉 as a function of gate duration τ for various number of ions. All ( n 2 ) gates are solved for their simultaneous implementation and the pulse power requirement ḡ is averaged over all gates.

TABLE VI. Lamb-Dicke parameters for thirteen ions.

FIG. 1. Calculated norm of pulse function g for parallel gate pulses as a function of gate indices, ordered according to the maximum weight matching problem solution, with the gate time specified on each plot. Fourier basis functions used span the frequency range of (ω1/(2π) − 100kHz, (ωN/(2π) + 100kHz), where ω1 and ωN are the lowest and highest motional mode frequencies, respectively. For visual clarity only half of the gate solutions are plotted.

FIG. 2. (a) Average calculated norm of pulse functions 〈g〉 of parallel gate pulses as a function of the number of ions N for different numbers of gates parallelized, with τ = 2000µs; Average was performed over all instances of the unique gate pattern studied. Squares: Parallelizing all ( N 2 ) gates as shown in the graph pattern example (b). Crosses: Parallelizing ⌊n/2⌋ disjoint gates as shown in the graph pattern example (c). Circles: Parallelizing two disjoint gates. A solid line (〈ḡ〉∼N) and a dashed line (〈ḡ〉∼ √ N) are provided to guide the eye.

FIG. 4. Circuits and experimental results for four-qubit HS and five-qubit BV. (a)-(b): Circuits for HS, BV, respectively. The dashed boxes represent parallelized gates in our experiments. (c) shows output state probabilities for the HS algorithm for serial (left) and parallel (right) implementations. (d) shows equivalent plots to (c), but for the BV algorithm. Note the color scale in non-linear for clarity.

FIG. 3. Parity scans for two disjoint gates with serial implementation (a), parallel implementation (b), and two gates with one overlapping ion for both serial and parallel (c). A parity scan is conducted by first implementing the desired XX gates then appending single-qubit π 2 rotations onto every qubit and varying the axis of rotations along the equator of the Bloch Sphere. We fit the data to y = A sin(νπφ+ φ0), where A is the amplitude or contrast of the parity scan that we use to estimate the fidelity of the gate, ν = 2 for (a) and (b) and ν = 3 for (c), φ is the analysis phase, and φ0 is a phase offset. Grey symbols: parity between qubits over pairs not intended to be entangled. Error bars are smaller than plot symbols.
